title: Dr. Aviv Regev, Broad Institute of MIT & Harvard

everyone so it's really a enormous
pleasure to introduce aiv as our second
keynote speaker of this meeting um I had
to realize when you're organizing this
conference you actually have very little
or no power because you completely
depend on these abstracts that come in
so the only thing you can actually do um
that that we as three organizers could
do is is pick a couple of keyote

one thing you do and I think you've seen
an example of that
is you take one of the old heroes or the

who's been over the years really driving

and has made continuous contributions
and that you've followed over over the
20 years this meeting exists and then of
course uh you can also take your new
Heroes and I'm absolutely thrilled that
we've decided to come here uh she's
really doing absolutely spectacular work
uh on that that I think is a is an
enormous inspiration for where actually
molecular Neuroscience has to go uh and
what her background is she's a
computational biologist to begin with so
she um was in studying Tel Aviv in a
interdisciplinary program that combined
computer science physics math and
biology uh then became a Bowell at
Harvard and then went to the broad
Institute where she's now the the chair
of the broad Institute and uh initially
she's really largely used computation uh
to look at a really complex forms of
Gene regulation dynamics of gene
expression um with with a computational
power to find nodes and signaling
networks that matter and to essentially
let the data tell you what's there
rather than you having some hypothesis
and what should it be and then trying to
um press the gain and loss of function
out of that factor that you decided to
marry and then make it have some effect
but actually to look at the data and let
the data inform you what is the most
important in regulating a
process um so she's really a Pioneer in
developing computational tools uh she
then also has built really a big branch
of her own um experimental science where
they're doing remarkable time resolve
gene expression analysis proteomics um
to look at how cells respond to stimuli
how um stimuli change gene expression
networks and thereby change function of
cells and much of this was done in cells
from the immune system but you can of
course already imagine that this is
actually fundamental to what we're
trying to figure out in a developing
nervous system on how a cell integrates
many different signals from the outside
how it then changes gene expression
pattern thereby changes function of the
cells and of the circuits and the type
of um analyses that that a has really
pioneered um looking at the level of
cell populations but also looking at the
level of single cells will I think
really be
um The Way Forward uh in understanding
how the brain develops and how circuits
develop um the nervous system that's why
why we felt that she would really be a a
fantastic person to have as a keynote
speaker here and so we're we're really
thrilled that she accepted to come to a
meeting that is sort of a little bit
outside of your your daily life uh um
but I think it will be an inspiration
for everyone who's here so thanks very
much for coming thank you so much for
superbly lovely
introduction I should put a disclaimer
up front because otherwise it will be
extremely nervous when the questions
come up I am not a neuroscientist and I
probably could not have followed most of
the talks in the meeting because this is
really not my field as Peter said I'm a
computational biologist by training I
study uh molecular system especially
molecular circuits within cells although
increasingly in the last few years the
circuitry between cells and our Focus
for the last decade or so really has
been mostly cells from the immune system
but I did always think that the nervous
system is not that unusual I think we
have three major distributed sensing
systems in the body there's the immune
system there's the nervous system and
there are the fibr blasts so at the end
of the day it's probably going to be
related to each other um no one told the
body that you should you know separate
all these things from each other um
having said that in the last few years
we've actually had some interesting
adventures in Neuroscience mostly
because of our increasing fascination
with thinking again about what can we
say about what I would say has been a
300 year old project to map the cells
from which multicellular organisms are
made but in particular to build a human
cell Atlas and this project started with
a discovery of cells when people had the
microscope in their hands and whenever
technology advanced in biology so has
our ability to Define what the cell
types are and what their functions might
be and we know that the cell's identity
has many different facets to it starting
with the well- defined types and then to
states that change dynamically
dependence on their location in absolute
coordinates in gradients but also just
the neighborhood of other cells around
them and the interactions with those
cells it dynamically shifts through
transitions especially in development
and there is an entire lineage for any
cell in our body that delineates
specifically the entire series of cell
division events that have led to this
cell from the zygote and so it's been a
long-term Endeavor and every time that a
new technology came on the um came on
the stage there's been a dramatic shift
with what we could do in this endeavor
and a new technology that really emerged
in the last few years has been the
ability to profile genomic profiles and
especially to profile the RNA content of
individual cells which I will call
single cell genomics or single cell RNA
seek and today we have in our hands as a
community both a core technology
involving specific molecular biology
techniques to do this profiling we have
increasingly good method meod to prepare
samples especially from large numbers of
cells we have a good and growing
computational toolbox to infer from
these profiles what might be going on
and the scale has really increased
dramatically especially in the last year
year and a half or so such that today we
can routinely analyze tens of thousands
and even more cells in a single day with
single cell RNA seek and so the
availability of these techniques has
really led the community to start
thinking about making a new or a refined
version of a human cell Atlas but when
we think about the application of these
kinds of methods to neuronal diversity
there are some specific challenges so
the first challenge is that in the
nervous system this is also true others
but it might be particularly through in
the nervous system it's not just that
there is a large and unknown number of
cell types but that we have a good
reason to expect that they would vary
orders of magnitude from each other so
they would be very abundant cells but
they would also be important and very
rare cell types Second Challenge is and
this is quite unique to the nervous
system it's one of the worst issues to
work with if what you want to recover is
individual intact cells and especially
in adult brain this becomes almost
impossible if not completely impossible
so if you want to do single cell
analysis and you can't recover single
cells that might be a problem um the
third challenge is that um some of the
unique populations even if they know
that they exist if we know that they
exist might require specific ways to
capture them one might want to think
about for example neurogenesis and the
availability of rarely dividing cells um
in the brain and then finally in
Neuroscience more so than in other
fields there is an entire scheme of cell
classification and description and
taxonomy which is independent of
molecular profiles or at least has never
been harmonized with them it might
depend on morphology it could depend on
the connectivity of cells to each other
and ultimately to the function that
these cells perform
and and very often we might ask
ourselves would molecular profiles
actually be congruent and harmonious
with these alternative definitions that
are attached with most of our Legacy
knowledge of how the system works and so
today I'm going to describe to you our
work to tackle some of these challenges
and I'm going to touch throughout the
talks on three different methods one is
called drop seek and it allows us to
perform massively parallel single cell
RNA seek the second method snook seek
allows us to avoid tissue dissociation
by analyzing nuclei instead of cells and
finally I'll describe div seik which we
use in order to capture very very rare
and newly born neurons and I'm going to
show you the specific examples where we
have applied this in the Neuroscience
realm and also talk a little bit about
the computational methods that we've
developed in order to infer out the
signals that we were most interested in
and I'm going to start with a gold
standard of neuronal cell identity which
is the mouse retina because a lot is is
known about the M retina it goes back to
Ramon kahal which is an example of the
invention of a new technology in this
case this and the ability to stain cells
and the dramatic impact that the ability
to stain had on how we Define cell
identity and so we reason that this
could be an excellent place to start
because especially with computational
people if we want to develop an
algorithm and we want to apply to data
and we want some answer at the end we
had better have a gold standard to
compare to to know whether we're on the
right track or not and we're going to
use all the Legacy knowledge of the
retina for this purpose and so our first
step was to develop a massively parallel
strategy for single cell RNA seek this
method is called drops it was published
more than over a year ago I'll go
through it very quickly imagine that you
can dissociate um a tissue obtain your
cells suspend those cells in little
droplets these are water droplets in a
medium of oil with surfactant that
stabilizes them so it's like reverse
salad dressing with with cells inside
the droplets and in the droplets you
also have barcoded beads where every
bead has both um has a unique um has a
unique barcode which characterizes that
bead in many many many many different
copies and so that would give um that
once um we blow up the cells inside
those droplets the RNA of those cells
will be captured on the beads and will
get barcoded by these unique barcodes
and then once it is actually barcoded we
can collect all of those beads together
which you can either stash them
somewhere and come back to them later or
we can grab a bunch of them and make one
just one RNA seek library but we know
the original cell from which each of the
RNA molecules and through that from
which each of the reads actually came
and this is actually what gives us that
scale that we can process you know these
tens of thousands of cells per day
instead of having to laboriously analyze
100 and so our first proof of concept
was to take whole retina dissociate it
we did it across seven different
biological replicates we obtained
4488 cells and then we used a
computational method I'm not going to
elaborate on we don't use it as much
today anymore in order to group the
cells what I do want to explain is how
to read this plot this is a nonlinear
embedding called tastic neighborhood
embedding where um you can think of it
as a PCA but it is no longer linear as
PCA is and so these are two Dimensions
each of the points is a cell and and
cells whose expression profile is
similar in high dimensional space are
also going to be close to each other in
this low dimensionality embedding so it
retains these local distances between
the cells you can see that they're
colored here the color is derived by a
computational algorithm that clusters
the cells into cluster in into clusters
in completely unsupervised way and now
we can take the cells in each of the
Clusters and we can ask whether there
are molecular markers that characterize
those cells and given those molecular
markers we can say oh do they actually
conform to what say you know somebody
who actually understands something about
the retina such as Jor SS our
collaborator would consider to be
independent cell types and in this
initial analysis in fact the 39 clusters
matched quite well into the known cell
types and so we felt kind of good that
we're not totally off track in these
kinds of Endeavors and more specifically
if we look at the major class es that
were that are known to exist in the
retina both of neurons and of Le cells
then we obtained roughly the correct
proportions of the expected cell types
and in those cell types where we expect
to have subtypes then in some cases like
bipolar cells or Amrine cells we get
about half as many clusters as the known
number of subtypes and those clusters
actually separate quite well relative to
known markers but also if you look at a
cell type like retinal gangan cells
where there should be dozens of
different subtypes in fact there was
only one cluster and there that was not
a surprise to us this was because our
analysis was completely unbiased
experimentally speaking and so retinal
ganglial cells might have many subtypes
but there are also the rarest subtype of
the retina now we just took whole retina
70% is rods and so 70% of the cells were
rods and so only few of the cells were
retinal gangan cells and this was not
enough to even capture all the subtypes
let alone identify them as distinguisher
on and so after a good proof of concept
feeling that the method works and this
should be successful we said well we're
not going to continuously spend 70% of
everything on rods let's do a little bit
of enrichment and so the next step was
to use um transgenic lines in order to
pre- enrich just for the broad cell
clusters cell classes and what I'm going
to focus on today is the first class
that we went after which are bipolar
cells with the first goal being reaching
a complete and validated and full

bipolar cells come in two major classes
there are cone bipolar cells and Rod

nevertheless these are the best
classified interneurons in the in the

there's another another thing that's
rare and we didn't know was there also
we can ask once we get transcriptional

that was the general idea in addition if

based on molecular information that it

that was

different clustering algorithms and
